item.title: Inside Ukraine’s fight to survive
item.description: Is there an end in sight for the Russia-Ukraine war? In this episode of “Make It Make Sense,” Kate Andrews asks Damir Marusic and Dominic Pino what they saw on the ground in Ukraine. They also discuss how Kyiv is employing drone warfare, why Vladimir Putin has resisted a peace deal and what it would take to bring Russia back to the negotiating table.

item.title: ‘Blood and soil’ nationalists don’t believe in American exceptionalism
item.description: The blood-and-soil right has more in common with the left than it wants to admit.

item.title: A toast to America at 250, glowing and decaying all at once
item.description: The country still dazzles the world. But it no longer leads it the way it did.

item.title: Penalty kicks are arbitrary and beautiful — just like life
item.description: Dumb luck decides the World Cup’s most thrilling moment. It decides a lot more, too.

item.title: Most polls miss the truth about the American Dream. Not this one.
item.description: In their own lives, most Americans still find the American Dream.

item.title: College-educated Americans need to unlearn this lesson about patriotism
item.description: There are two definitions of America. Both have a place.

item.title: This iconic painting went full ‘Bridgerton’ on the Founding Fathers
item.description: John Trumbull’s 1819 artwork turned fashionable peacocks into plain men.

item.title: The not-so-hidden costs of giving Iran $300 billion
item.description: A richer Iranian regime means a more violent one. The West will need to beef up its security.

item.title: Abolish the NIH
item.description: A $48 billion government monopoly should give way to the market.

item.title: The FAA clears the runway to revive supersonic travel
item.description: Advances in engineering and flying at high altitudes can mitigate noise and avoid the rattling blast of a sonic boom.

item.title: Democrats are facing their MAGA moment. Good luck containing it.
item.description: The socialist surge mirrors the past GOP takeover — and the establishment lost that one, too.

item.title: I love my husband, but my marriage is costing me
item.description: An outdated tax code forces dual-income couples to pay a premium.

item.title: At 250, America faces its third trial
item.description: The nation’s semiquincentennial requires a declaration of potential.

item.title: A 264-year-old George Washington mystery solved
item.description: A military trail transforms into a highway of American progress.

item.title: David Petraeus: What a wave of prominent arrests means for Iraq
item.description: The anti-corruption push hints at the stronger state the region needs — but big tests remain.

item.title: The weed killer America can’t afford to lose
item.description: A 7-2 Supreme Court decision protects Bayer from costly litigation over Roundup. Thank goodness.

item.title: What happened when fashion moved online
item.description:
There was a time when shopping for clothes meant walking into a department store and hoping the limited sizes and styles available at that exact moment might fit. Then, the internet changed everything. Online shopping didn’t just make fashion more convenient; it expanded what retailers could offer, what customers could demand and who got to dictate trends.
Host Megan McArdle is joined by Shawn Grain Carter, a professor at the Fashion Institute of Technology and former Bloomingdale’s buyer who helped launch the Macy’s website. They discuss the shift from department store floors to the infinite shelves of the internet.

item.title: The birthright citizenship ruling isn’t a catastrophe
item.description: The government has plenty of mechanisms to discourage birth tourism and illegal immigration.

item.title: Is this the Democratic tea party? | Liam Donovan
item.description: Is the Democratic Party having its tea party moment? In this episode of “Make It Make Sense,” James Hohmann talks with Liam Donovan, political strategist and a president at Targeted Victory, about the rise of Democratic populism, its effects on the midterms and how it compares to the rise of Trumpism.
